data.title: A ‘final piano song’ offers closure as residents say goodbye to Wang Fuk Court
data.description:
Playing one last song on a piano that cannot be retrieved from a fire-ravaged home is among the wishes of residents returning to the scene of the deadly Tai Po blaze in Hong Kong, with one man climbing stairs on a fractured leg to try and recover a water boiler linked to his childhood memories.
Some of those returning to their homes on Friday had lived in Wang Cheong House, where 81 of the 168 deaths occurred and 63 per cent of the flats were destroyed.
Only five floors of the block are being opened each day over a six-day period that ends on Tuesday.
Others are from Wang Yan House, where residents are returning in batches over three days until Saturday.
A Wang Cheong House resident surnamed Kwok suffered a fractured foot in the fire but was prepared to hobble up seven floors to his former home.
Kwok said he had asked a social worker whether he could return at a later date after his recovery but was told only that authorities would make proactive arrangements.

Fearing he might miss the opportunity, he decided to make the ascent with the help of friends and a folding chair.
“What I want to retrieve most is a water boiler, which I have had since I was a teenager,” Kwok said. “My father cleaned it several times with soda and lemon to keep it in good condition.”
Kwok’s father died a year ago, and he has been trying to recover items belonging to him. He said he particularly wanted to see the dining room, where his father used to sit and watch television.
A resident of Wang Yan House surnamed Woo, who lived on a middle floor, expressed deep fear about returning to her flat.
“I am so frightened to go back,” she said with tears, adding she would only return if accompanied by friends.
“But I hope to go up one last time to play a farewell song on the piano. This is my way of saying goodbye to my home.”
She said it was incredibly difficult to pack up almost her entire life’s belongings in just three hours.

Earlier, Mandy Wong, a resident of Wang Sun House, told the South China Morning Post that although her flat had blackened walls, scorched window frames and a peeling kitchen ceiling, her piano and sheet music remained intact.
The instrument’s red velvet cover retained its sheen and colour, standing out starkly against the charred surroundings, she added.
She also managed to retrieve her cello.
After packing, Kwok told the SCMP he had filled about 13 bags with belongings, adding he could hardly describe his emotions on returning home.
“It was unexpected. When I looked at the window where I used to wait for my father to come home when I was very young, I froze. I didn’t know how to describe my feelings,” he said.

He added that the flat was half-empty, as his father had died before the fire. Still, he managed to salvage several keepsakes, including a collection of vinyl records.
Kwok also considered taking his father’s old chest freezer. Because his father had mobility issues in his later years, the freezer had been essential for storing food.
However, given its size and his lack of storage space, he decided to leave it behind.
Ultimately, he could not find the water boiler and believed his father might have discarded it.
Under the 15-day programme that began on Monday, Wang Fuk Court residents are allowed three hours in their flats, excluding time spent on the stairs.
Over the past few days, residents have been seen carrying suitcases, nylon bags and cardboard boxes as they try to salvage appliances and keepsakes.

Each resident is accompanied by a police officer, with up to four people per household allowed to return at a time. However, only one or two may enter severely damaged flats deemed unsafe.
The inferno, which lasted about 43 hours and ravaged all but one block, was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948 and displaced nearly 5,000 people.

data.title: Huawei earmarks US$11.7b for training of autonomous-driving systems
data.description:
Adamant about retaining its runaway lead in supplying smart driving systems in China, Huawei Technologies plans to invest as much as 80 billion yuan (US$11.7 billion) over the next five years to boost computing power essential for training and testing semi-autonomous cars.
The massive capital expenditure would enhance the reliability of cars fitted with the Huawei Qiankun ADS autopilot system, as the Shenzhen-based tech giant looked to expand its customer base, said Jin Yuzhi, CEO of Huawei’s intelligent automotive solutions business unit.
In 2026 alone, Huawei would earmark 18 billion yuan for research and development in autonomous driving, he added.
“Autonomous driving is an industry requiring long-term investment,” Jin said at an event in Beijing on Thursday. “Based on our investment of 18 billion yuan in smart driving this year, we will spend more than the combined expense of all other major [autonomous driving] solution providers.”
Computing power plays a key role in improving self-driving capabilities by processing data collected during training and testing of advanced driver assistance systems (ADASs).
Huawei’s Qiankun ADS platform surpassed 10 billion kilometres of autonomous driving early this month.
Autopilot features powered by the platform are widely used in mainland China. Huawei has partnerships with 25 car brands, and Qiankun has been installed in more than 50 models. A total of 1.7 million vehicles fitted with the system have been delivered to Chinese customers.
Aside from Huawei, only US electric vehicle (EV) maker Tesla claims to have amassed as much real-world data.
Some Chinese ADAS systems have proved superior to Tesla’s Full Self-Driving (FSD) navigation software on mainland roads, according to David Zhang, general secretary of the Shanghai-based International Intelligent Vehicle Engineering Association.
However, the Chinese self-driving technologies are so far limited to the domestic market, and FSD is the front-runner internationally, he added.
Huawei supplies domestic carmakers such as Arcfox and Avatr with automotive chips, lidar (light detection and ranging) sensors and digital technologies that allow cars to be semi-autonomous and linked to the internet.
It ventured into the car industry in 2021 through a partnership with Chongqing-based carmaker Seres. Aito, an EV venture between the two companies, is now one of the bestselling premium EV brands in China.

In mid-2024, Seres agreed to pay 2.5 billion yuan to buy the Aito ­trademark and patents from Huawei, a deal that reflected Huawei’s determination to enhance its role as a supplier of vehicle systems and know-how, rather than an EV maker, according to analysts.
Huawei’s pitch to the automotive industry is that it can help its partners cut development costs, accelerate new model launches and improve efficiency in testing smart-driving systems.
Nearly all major carmakers in China are now actively developing cars with “hands off” self-driving features to gain an edge in the cutthroat market.
In December, Beijing gave the first carmakers the go-ahead to build EVs with level 3 (L3) autonomous driving capability. On an international scale from L0 to L5, an L3 autonomous vehicle can operate independently under predefined conditions, although the driver must remain ready at all times to take control if required.

data.title: China’s premium EVs mount tech-powered challenge to BMW, Mercedes-Benz at expo10-day show in Beijing, the world’s largest, to feature 1,451 models and exhibition area of 380,000 square metres.
data.description:
This year’s Auto China, the world’s largest car show, is likely to mark a watershed for Chinese electric vehicle (EV) makers as they push dozens of new premium models into the spotlight, challenging European manufacturers such as BMW and Mercedes-Benz.
Leading EV makers in mainland China, from BYD and Geely to Leapmotor and Nio, will showcase their latest models at the 10-day show, which starts in Beijing on Friday, having moved up the value chain through the rapid adoption of technological advances.
“Chinese EV brands will feel confident in promoting their new models priced above 300,000 yuan (US$43,978),” said Qian Kang, who runs a vehicle circuit board factory in Zhejiang province. “They will make the most of the Beijing auto show to convey the message to the global automotive industry and consumers around the world.”
The show’s organisers say 1,451 models will be displayed at the China International Exhibition Centre and the Capital International Exhibition Centre – the two venues for the event – until May 3. The exhibition area will be nearly 50 per cent bigger than it was at the previous show in 2024, hitting 380,000 square metres (4.09 million sq ft).
Among the vehicles to be exhibited in Beijing, 181 will be making their global debuts.
EVs priced below 100,000 yuan (US$14,647) have been a key growth driver for the Chinese car market since 2023, with subsidies helping to attract thousands of young and low-income customers.
About 7 million cars priced between 100,000 yuan and 150,000 yuan – including petrol-powered and electric models – were sold on the mainland in 2024, accounting for a third of the market by volume, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association.
However, since the middle of last year, Beijing’s efforts to discourage an EV discount war and instead push carmakers to focus on technological innovation to bolster sales have prompted a pivot away from low-priced models to premium ones.
Nick Lai, head of auto research for the Asia-Pacific region at JPMorgan, said carmakers were now taking one of two paths: upgrading production techniques to reduce manufacturing costs and make their prices more attractive, or offering consumers new models that outperformed existing ones at the same price.
Tian Maowei, a sales ­manager at Yiyou Auto Service in Shanghai, said: “Wealthy consumers took a cue from the government directives, hunting for value-for-money electric cars whose intelligent features were much more advanced than those of established luxury brands. All the big players are resorting to premium models to improve profitability now that the brutal price war has come to an end.”
German marques BMW and Mercedes-Benz dominated the mainland’s premium car segment over the past two decades, but BMW reported a 12.5 per cent fall in car sales on the mainland last year, while Mercedes-Benz saw its sales slump 19.5 per cent.
William Li Bin, CEO of Shanghai-based EV maker Nio, said last month that it had outsold BMW in Shanghai and other developed cities in the fourth quarter of last year.
Nio will promote its new fully electric ES9 SUV, which features its self-developed five-nanometre Shenji driving assistance chip, at the Beijing show. The SUV has a 102-kilowatt-hour battery pack that gives it a driving range of 620km (385 miles).
Aito, an EV brand backed by Huawei Technologies, will unveil its refreshed M9 SUV, a luxury vehicle fitted with six lidar sensors and a digital cockpit.
Shenzhen-based carmaker BYD’s second-generation Great Tang SUV will showcase its superfast charging technology, which the company says will ease drivers’ range anxiety by allowing them to drive 830km after nine minutes of charging.
Analysts said China’s carmakers and its companies in the automotive supply chain stand at the vanguard of EV technology and production, buoyed by government support and consumers’ willingness to embrace innovation.
On Friday, Hesai Group, the world’s largest maker of vehicle lidar sensors, introduced technology it said would raise autonomous driving features to a new level by detecting colours to improve the reliability of object identification.
On Tuesday, Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd, the world’s largest EV battery producer, announced that its sodium-ion batteries, an alternative to lithium-ion batteries, would be mass-produced and delivered in the fourth quarter of this year.
Sodium-ion technology uses a more widely available raw material and the batteries are cheaper to produce.

data.title: Tai Po probe: fire chief says authorities could have collaborated better
data.description:
At the start of the inquiry, committee lead counsel Victor Dawes identified six “human factors” that led to the blaze’s heavy death toll.
These included the use of flammable polyfoam boards to seal household windows, the use of allegedly non-fire-retardant scaffolding mesh, the wooden movable boards installed on emergency staircases that allowed easier access to scaffolding and replaced fireproof windows and workers’ smoking habits.
Other failures were the deactivation of the fire alarm system and the drained fire service tanks.

data.title: Ex-manager of crypto firm Huobi acquitted of moving millions in Tether account
data.description:
A former senior manager at a major Chinese cryptocurrency exchange has been acquitted by a Hong Kong court of secretly moving five million Tether, with a market value of about HK$39 million (US$5 million), to his own accounts six years ago.
A seven-member High Court jury reached the verdict on Friday to find Chen Boliang, who used to work at cryptocurrency firm Huobi – now known as HTX – not guilty of accessing its platform to trade in the dark pool under an individual account he created with an alias between February 27 and March 14, 2020.
According to the Securities and Futures Commission’s regulations, only institutional investors are allowed to use the dark pool, while individual investors are strictly prohibited.
Dark pool trading involves institutional investors trading privately without publicly disclosing the transactions, the buy and sell prices and the number of shares traded.
Prosecutors alleged that Chen set up a Huobi account under the name of “Chen Feng” in January 2020.
One month later, Chen began using that individual account, which was registered with a mainland identification card, and three other company accounts over which he had control on Huobi to trade in the dark pool.
After a day of deliberation, the jury returned a majority verdict finding insufficient evidence to prove that the defendant was the controller of the “Chen Feng” account, as evidence showed that his accounts on other crypto exchanges – Binance and OKX – had received a large number of cryptocurrency transfers from the Huobi accounts over the period in question.

The transfers involved a total of five million Tether, with an estimated market value of about HK$39 million.
Chen was also acquitted of six counts of access to a computer with criminal or dishonest intent and another count of money laundering.
Four former executives of Huobi, including its ex-COO and an audit director, refused to testify for the prosecution in the High Court trial.
Senior counsel Derek Chan Ching-lung applied for his client to be compensated for legal costs after the prosecution failed to prove its case.
The request was granted by the judge.
Huobi began operations in mainland China in 2013 but pulled out of the country amid Beijing’s cryptocurrency crackdown in 2021. It was rebranded as HTX in 2023 and is now headquartered in the Seychelles.

data.title: Breaking|DeepSeek releases next-gen AI model with ‘world-leading’ cost efficiency
data.description:
DeepSeek has finally released its much-anticipated next-generation foundational artificial intelligence model, the open-source V4, which it said was competitive with leading US closed-source models from the likes of OpenAI and Google DeepMind.
DeepSeek released two versions of the model on Friday, with the V4-pro model boasting 1.6 trillion parameters, making it the Hangzhou start-up’s biggest ever model by that metric, while the smaller V4-flash model has 284 billion parameters.
Both models have a context window of 1 million tokens, a critical feature that determines the amount of information an AI system is able to process, which DeepSeek said was achieved with “world-leading” cost efficiency.
More to follow...

data.title: Chinese EV makers bet on in-house chips to make cars smarter and more autonomous
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle (EV) makers and supply-chain vendors, from Tesla rivals Xpeng to Nio and lidar sensor producer Hesai Group, will display their technological advances on a new front – in-car chips – at the Auto China show in Beijing as the country’s automotive industry makes rapid progress.
Carmakers and vendors are designing and developing chips to power autonomous driving and in-car entertainment systems. These chips boast computing power on par with blockbuster products made by chip giant Nvidia, and are expected to add lustre to Chinese-made EVs that are rapidly gaining popularity globally.
“If we use chips provided by third-party suppliers, we cannot guarantee that the chips would cater to our needs in developing lidar sensors,” said David Li Yifan, CEO of Hesai, the world’s largest maker of lidar sensors for cars, at a media briefing last week. “We want to design and make the chips ourselves because no one else specifically develops chiplets for a lidar maker.”
Computing power has been in high demand over the past decade as Chinese EV makers have doubled down on smart vehicles, banking on artificial intelligence and digital technologies to make vehicles more autonomous and comfortable.
A system-on-chip (SoC) that processes data from a vehicle’s cameras and sensors offers strong computing power to support rapidly evolving intelligent features.
Xpeng’s indigenous Turing chip, designed for level 4 autonomous driving capabilities, was three times more powerful than Nvidia’s Drive Orin X installed in its existing smart vehicles, according to He Xiaopeng, CEO of the Guangzhou-based EV maker.

The Turing chip has a computing power of 750 tera ­operations per second – a measure of how many trillion operations it can perform per second.
The Shenji self-driving chip, developed by Nio, had computing power equal to four Orin X chips, according to the Shanghai-based EV maker.
“We hope our advanced chips will reach out to more partners in the field of physical AI in future,” said William Li, CEO of Nio, last month. “Our chips are very powerful and can benefit other companies.”
Amid the China-US tech war, Beijing has been encouraging domestic firms to cut reliance on imported semiconductors while building a resilient domestic supply chain.
Analysts said China’s carmakers and automotive suppliers were at the vanguard of EV technology and production, buoyed by government support and consumer appetite for innovation.
In November, Germany’s Volkswagen Group partnered with Horizon Robotics, a Chinese AI chip designer, to develop advanced semiconductors for its locally made semi-autonomous vehicles.
Ralf Brandsatter, chairman and CEO of Volkswagen China, said the move aimed to accelerate the carmaker’s localisation drive as it hoped to master core EV technologies.
Xpeng, which also develops flying cars and humanoid robots, bills itself as an AI company.
“For AI, if you want to deliver better performance, you need computing capability,” said Jacky Gu Jie, a vice-president at Xpeng. “In a world where technology is rapidly evolving, self-development will improve efficiency, creating technological value for a firm.”
In late 2025, Xpeng CEO He said that the goal was to sell 1 million robots by 2030, pledging to bring down production costs so that robots could become affordable for households.
The remarks were made just days after Tesla CEO Elon Musk endorsed “Iron”, Xpeng’s next-generation robot, on social media and predicted Chinese companies would ­dominate the global robotics market alongside Tesla.
Last Friday, Hesai unveiled its latest technology to produce 6D full-colour light ­detection and ranging ­sensors. 6D refers to a sensor’s ability to detect the X, Y and Z coordinates of an object, plus its reflectivity, velocity and colour.
Hesai said the next-generation lidar sensors would work with the company’s self-developed Picasso SoC chips. The chips would also be used in its new Kosmo handheld device, which the company described as a “physical AI eye” for capturing and recording space in 3D.
The Beijing car show, the world’s largest, will be held simultaneously in two venues – the China International Exhibition Centre and the Capital International Exhibition Centre – from Friday to May 3.

data.title: Tech war: US Congress rolls out ‘largest’ export control upgrade against China
data.description:
US lawmakers have advanced 20 new export control measures – including the controversial Match Act – to further restrict Chinese access to US technology and bar Chinese chipmakers from gaining access to advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment.
In the latest salvo in the US-China tech war, the House Foreign Affairs Committee on Wednesday passed the measures through the committee stage to be deliberated by the rest of the House, describing the action as the “largest significant export control mark-up in the history of Congress”.
“China has made it abundantly clear that it intends to dominate the technologies that underpin both our economy and our national defence,” said Republican Representative Michael Baumgartner. “The United States cannot afford to leave open back doors that allow the Chinese Communist Party to acquire the tools it needs to leap ahead in semiconductor manufacturing.”

The Match Act proposed by Baumgartner, which stands for Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware, would require US allies such as Japan and the Netherlands to more closely align with US restrictions on selling advanced semiconductor equipment to China, such as Dutch firm ASML’s deep ultraviolet (DUV) immersion lithography machines.
Introduced in early April, the initial bill raised alarm among industry players both in the US and in allied countries over concerns that the new expansive restrictions would hurt sales.
Some of the restrictions were rolled back before Wednesday’s vote, including a countrywide ban on selling cryogenic etching tools for making chips to China, though restrictions on DUV machine exports remained, according to a Reuters report.
“This bill would address one of the gravest issues we face today, by limiting China’s access to the most critical machines and parts needed to make advanced chips,” said Brian Mast, the House Foreign Affairs Committee chairman, on Wednesday.
Other bills advanced on Wednesday include the Stop Stealing Our Chips Act, which would establish a programme to reward whistle-blowers who report export control violations, and the Deterring American AI Model Theft Act, which would authorise sanctions against Chinese artificial intelligence model companies accused of distilling US model outputs.
The US chip industry is a major backer of greater restrictions on China.
On Wednesday, Reuters reported that Micron Technology, the biggest US memory chipmaker, was lobbying US lawmakers to pass the Match Act to stop its Chinese competitors such as ChangXin Memory Technologies, Yangtze Memory Technologies and Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corp from using advanced equipment to produce chips.

data.title: AI-driven Hong Kong stock inflows from mainland China slow as options multiplyWave of listings provides more ‘pure-play’ AI investment options across mainland and Hong Kong exchanges, BNP Paribas says
data.description:
Mainland Chinese investors have slowed their purchases of Hong Kong-listed shares this year after last year’s record inflows, as more artificial intelligence investment opportunities have emerged in mainland markets, according to BNP Paribas.
Southbound inflows via the Stock Connect cross-border system have reached about US$30 billion so far this year, a slower pace than 2025, when they hit US$180 billion for the full year, according to the French bank. The deceleration reflected changing market dynamics rather than a retreat from Chinese assets, BNP Paribas strategists said at a media briefing on Wednesday.
“Investors are still positive on China’s AI story,” said Jason Lui, head of Asia-Pacific equity and derivative strategy, adding that the difference this year was that they had “more options to express those views”.
Last year’s rally was driven by the sudden emergence of AI start-up DeepSeek, which caught markets by surprise and led to concentrated buying of Hong Kong-listed technology giants, he said. This year had no event of similar magnitude, while a wave of new listings over the past six to nine months had provided more pure-play AI investment options across both mainland and Hong Kong markets.
As a result, investors were increasingly shifting from broad index exposure to more selective bets on individual companies, reducing the need to rely on Hong Kong-listed internet stocks as proxies for the AI theme, according to the bank.
The slowdown in southbound inflows also reflected a higher base after last year’s surge, said Kenny Tang Sing-hing, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Financial Analysts and Professional Commentators.
The shift was closely tied to the AI investment theme, as many of the most sought-after AI and chip companies had already secured funding from mainland cornerstone and strategic investors during their pre-IPO stages.
“A lot of the capital has already gone into these companies before they list,” Tang said, noting that new shares were not accessible through the Stock Connect programme.
Despite weaker southbound inflows, trading activity in Hong Kong has remained resilient. The average daily turnover in March rose 8 per cent from a year earlier to HK$304 billion (US$39 billion), while the total market capitalisation of Hong Kong’s securities market reached HK$45.9 trillion at the end of March, a 15 per cent increase from a year earlier, stock exchange data showed.
The AI investment cycle continued to underpin the global equity market, according to BNP Paribas. Technology stocks, particularly those linked to hardware and supply chains, regained momentum in recent months, supported by strong pricing power and resilience to inflationary pressures, the bank added.
It also highlighted improving opportunities in mainland markets, particularly among mid-cap stocks. The CSI 500 index, which has a higher weighting in sectors such as technology, industrials and materials, was expected to benefit from Beijing’s push for technology improvement under the 15th five-year plan, the bank said.
Mid-cap stocks tended to be more sensitive to changes in producer prices, and a gradual recovery in the producer price index could improve their earnings outlook, BNP Paribas said. It added that the CSI 500 offered a blend of exposure to both technology and cyclical recovery, positioning it well in the current environment.

data.title: Man dies after fainting on Cathay Pacific flight from UK to Hong Kong
data.description:
A 51-year-old man who fainted on a Cathay Pacific flight to Hong Kong from the UK was pronounced dead on Friday morning.
Flight CX216 took off from Manchester and landed at Hong Kong International Airport at 6.35am on Friday.
Police said they received a report about the man’s collapse at 6.16am, adding that the deceased had a history of illness.
The South China Morning Post has reached out to the carrier for comment.

data.title: Hongkongers find value, space and style in Shenzhen’s new cultural hotspots
data.description:
Hongkonger Dominique Li and his wife spent a two-day break earlier this month in Shenzhen to soak up the cultural vibe, a short getaway they said would not be their last.
The 45-year-old engineer, who lives in Tin Shui Wai in the northwestern New Territories, was particularly drawn to a new landmark, Shenzhen Bay Culture Square in Nanshan district.
He treated his visit to Shenzhen like a trip to Hong Kong’s Causeway Bay shopping district, which also takes about an hour.
“The spaces here are bigger and there’s more choice. If you want high-end consumption you can find it, but there are also lots of cafes and more relaxed places where people can just sit and spend time,” Li said at the Shenzhen cultural plaza.
“It feels like there are always new things opening, so it gives you a reason to come back again.”

The couple are among droves of Hongkongers drawn back to Shenzhen because of its new, convenient and more economical offerings. Experts warn that Hong Kong must leverage its international status and unique events to counter the mainland city’s booming, budget-friendly attractions – or risk losing ground.
The new landmark – a 20-minute walk from the Shenzhen Bay border checkpoint – is a sprawling complex combining futuristic, white indoor spaces with lush, green grounds. Visitors move between exhibition halls, cafes and design stores, with Cantonese heard as frequently as Mandarin.
Holiday exodus
Waves of Hong Kong residents leaving the city during long holidays were again evident during Easter and the Ching Ming Festival earlier this month.
The Easter holiday spanned five days, from April 3 to 7, compared with four last year because it overlapped with the three-day Ching Ming Festival from April 4 to 6.
For an apples-to-apples comparison, Hongkongers made 2.12 million passenger trips out of the city in the first four days of Easter, 14.3 per cent more than in the same period last year. Over the four days, mainland visitors made 411,972 passenger trips to Hong Kong, a 16.13 per cent increase.
“The two cities complement each other, like Tokyo and Yokohama in Japan,” Timothy Chui Ting-pong, executive director of the Hong Kong Tourism Association, said when asked if the continuous new offerings by Shenzhen were what drew Hongkongers back.
“In Hong Kong, pop concerts, New Year countdowns, Disneyland, and events like the Rugby Sevens at Kai Tak Sport Park will also be part of life for Shenzhen residents.”

He noted that the central government had designated Hong Kong as the “East-meets-West centre for international cultural exchange”, so institutions and their events would need to cater not only to local audiences but also to those from overseas.
With a population of 20 million, Shenzhen already had sufficient demand to support its own arts and cultural industry, Chui said, meaning institutions and events there tended to be more locally focused.
“The very positioning [of Hong Kong as the international cultural exchange hub] could help prevent unnecessary competition or overlap of functions [between the two cities],” Chui said.
‘Very good value’
Shenzhen Bay Culture Square, designed by Beijing-based MAD Architects, opened last November and quickly became a popular attraction, as social media influencers rushed to post selfies from the site.
Its many photo spots, including a dimly lit tunnel illuminated by glowing walls, a central water feature and architectural elements defined by sweeping curves, make for postcard-like pictures.
Visitors have since flocked to the waterfront precinct, which has 180,800 square metres of gross floor area, including museum exhibition spaces, collection storage, theatres and auditoriums, as well as retail and dining outlets.
The complex has also drawn overseas visitors.
The Oh family of four, visiting from the United Kingdom, said they were in China taking advantage of the visa-free policy allowing stays of up to 30 days introduced in February this year.
They said they had spent several days in Shenzhen and Hong Kong, and found the mainland city compared favourably in some respects.
“It feels quite similar in some ways, but the service here is a lot better,” said mother Yan, adding that they had enjoyed spa and nail treatments as well as shopping, describing the overall experience as “very good value”.

The family said they had initially expected Shenzhen to feel more traditional, but were surprised by the range of modern attractions and would consider returning.
Australian influencers Archimarathon, comprising two architects, were impressed by the speed of infrastructure development, saying it took almost a year to obtain planning permits in their own country.
A video the pair made in January analysing the architecture of Shenzhen Bay Culture Plaza has been viewed 1.5 million times.
The complex is part of a broader push by Shenzhen to expand its cultural infrastructure since 2018, with 10 major facilities planned across the city.

Some, including the new Shenzhen Art Museum and Shenzhen Library North, opened in 2023, while construction of the Shenzhen Opera House, designed by Pritzker Prize-winning architect Jean Nouvel, began last year.
More are in the pipeline, including two privately funded museums backed by tech giants Tencent and JD.com, both slated to open in 2027.
Other relatively new landmarks in Shenzhen include Gangxia North subway station in Futian – known as the Eye of Shenzhen – the Zhongshuge bookshop with spiral bookshelves in Bao’an district, and the 4km-long red bridge in Hongqiao Park in Guangming district.
Can Hong Kong compete?
Hong Kong has also been investing in cultural infrastructure to boost tourism, with a mix of museums, performance venues and large-scale event spaces.
A focal point is the West Kowloon Cultural District, where the Hong Kong Palace Museum, which concentrates on Chinese artefacts, opened in 2023, and contemporary art museum M+ in 2022. The WestK Performing Arts Centre is scheduled for completion this year.
Elsewhere, the East Kowloon Cultural Centre opened last September, offering a range of venues, while the 50,000-seat Kai Tak Stadium has hosted concerts by acts such as Blackpink and Coldplay since opening last year.
Plans are also under way for Hong Kong’s first private “Broadway-calibre” theatre in Central, led by developer Henderson Land.
Not all initiatives have maintained momentum.
The reopening of the border town of Sha Tau Kok in June 2022 drew an initial surge in visitors, with group numbers peaking at more than 61,000 in 2024 before falling to about 33,000 last year, according to government figures.
Individual visits, introduced in 2024, totalled about 88,000 that year and slipped slightly to 83,000 in 2025, with locals accounting for 75 per cent of visitors.
Walk in Hong Kong co-founder and chief executive Paul Chan Chi-yuen said the decline was understandable as the novelty of the restricted area wore off.
“It’s enough for many to visit once, not many will head back,” Chan said, adding that the area could be packaged with island-hopping and destinations such as Lai Chi Wo or nearby hiking trails.
The application of permits was also a barrier for visitors as they might prefer more flexibility in their itinerary, he said, adding Hong Kong should focus on the quality of goods and services to maintain its competitive edge over rivals such as Shenzhen.

Simon Lee Siu-po, a finance lecturer at Hong Kong Shue Yan University, said Hong Kong should focus on attracting high-spending international visitors rather than just boosting tourist numbers.
“Hong Kong still has a competitive edge as an international city. There are not as many international flights to Shenzhen. It’s OK if a fraction of these visitors come via us to Shenzhen, but at least the majority are still coming to Hong Kong,” Lee said.
While offerings at institutions such as M+ and the Hong Kong Palace Museum were of high quality, many of their exhibits were borrowed, lowering the bar for other museums to catch up, he said.
A significant portion of exhibits at the Hong Kong Palace Museum are on rotation from its counterpart in Beijing, while overseas institutions such as the Palace of Versailles in France and the UK’s National Gallery have also brought collections to the city.
“If we go down this route to differentiate ourselves [from counterparts in Shenzhen], the costs could be very high, as with all things in Hong Kong … I have some reservations,” Lee said, highlighting low costs on the mainland and the support from casinos in Macau.
Hong Kong could be at a disadvantage if Shenzhen and Macau upped their game, he warned, stressing the need for long-running attractions that encouraged visitors to stay and spend. It could also take decades to build up a strong base of local artists and industry practitioners, he said.
For now, people still notice a difference in cultural offerings.
A Hong Kong couple, who gave their name as the Cheungs, said M+ and the Palace Museum were often “too crowded”.

The new Shenzhen complex was spacious and more leisurely to visit, Ms Cheung said, while Hong Kong museums “felt more upscale, while here it’s more casual”.
She said she often visited Shenzhen for food and services such as spas.
“I usually come up to eat and get a massage. There are some restaurants here that I really like, so I come back quite often,” she said. “It’s more like a short escape, even just for an afternoon, you can come up, have a meal and relax before heading back.”
She added that Shenzhen would not replace Hong Kong.
“They’re just different … Hong Kong is more for everyday life, while here feels more like a quick getaway.”
New local offerings in the pipeline
A spokesman for the Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau said Hong Kong continued to invest in both public and private cultural facilities.
He noted that Hong Kong Disneyland Resort was planning new Pixar- and Marvel-themed areas, which the park called “major expansion projects” in September last year. Ocean Park is set to open a new “Adventure Zone” in 2028 that features zip lines and bungee jumps.
Government projects in the pipeline include the Chinese Culture Experience Centre, a multipurpose professional performance venue in the New Territories, and a museum in Kwu Tung North showcasing the country’s development and achievements.
“While infrastructure and facilities provide the necessary venues for staging cultural programmes, it is the quality and uniqueness of the cultural offerings that determine a city’s appeal,” the spokesman said.
He added that the Hong Kong Pop Culture Festival, organised by the government, had attracted an increasing number of overseas attendees, rising from 15 per cent in the first two editions to 25 per cent in the latest, among a total audience of 2 million over three years.
Commercial annual cultural affairs, from pop culture festival ComplexCon to contemporary art fairs such as Art Basel, have also consistently brought in high-spending visitors from around the world.
The government has allocated HK$1.66 billion (US$212 million) to the Hong Kong Tourism Board to scale up flagship events – including sporting occasions – extend their durations and introduce new themed light festivals.
For many Hong Kong residents, Shenzhen’s appeal lies not in replacing the city’s institutions, but in offering a more relaxed, accessible alternative that makes repeat visits easy.
“Hong Kong felt a bit quiet over the holidays, and we’ve noticed there are more places here with a stronger cultural atmosphere, so we wanted to come explore,” engineer Li said.

data.title: All Hong Kong public universities make Asian league table top 100 for first time
data.description:
For the first time, all eight Hong Kong public universities have been ranked among the top 100 in a widely watched Asian league table, with the city’s two oldest tertiary institutions ranking among the region’s top 10.
Phil Baty, chief global affairs officer of Times Higher Education (THE), also highlighted Hong Kong’s role as a world-leading education hub, noting it remained the only metropolis with as many as five universities listed in the global top 100 since 2024, an achievement he said no other major cities, such as London or Boston, could match.
The British-based organisation published its Asia University Rankings on Thursday, with the University of Hong Kong retaining sixth place, while the Chinese University of Hong Kong dropped one position to 10th.
Meanwhile, the Education University of Hong Kong (EdUHK) placed 37th as the second-highest new entrant, with Lingnan University ranking 84th.
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST) maintained 12th place, followed by City University, which rose two places to 14th. Polytechnic University retained 18th place, while Baptist University recorded the biggest improvement among local institutions, climbing from 50th to 40th.


In the top 10, mainland China’s Tsinghua University held the top spot for the eighth consecutive year, while Peking University remained second for the seventh straight year. The National University of Singapore and Nanyang Technological University retained third and fourth places, respectively.
The University of Tokyo ranked fourth, climbing one place from last year.
In total, a record 929 universities from 36 countries and territories were ranked, up from 853 universities across 35 countries and territories last year.
Universities were assessed across five performance areas: teaching; research environment; research quality; international outlook, including staff, students and research; and industry income and patents.
At Wednesday’s Asia Universities Summit, co-hosted by THE and HKUST, Baty said Hong Kong would remain a flagship amid the region’s rise, cementing its status as a global powerhouse.
“There are eight universities from Hong Kong. Five are in the top 100. And let’s be clear, no other metropolis in the world, not London, not Paris, not Boston, not San Francisco, Singapore, Beijing, nowhere has more top 100 universities than Hong Kong,” he said.
Since 2024, HKU, CUHK, HKUST, PolyU and CityU have been ranked among the world’s top 100 universities by the publication.

EdUHK president John Lee Chi-kin said the recognition affirmed the institution’s commitment to advancing impactful research and fostering a dynamic international learning community.
“It is a testament to the dedication and collective efforts of our faculty and staff. Going forward, we will continue to enhance our research capacity as well as learning and teaching quality, strengthen collaborations across local, regional and global networks,” he said.
Joe Qin Sizhao, president of Lingnan University, said the result marked an important milestone in the institution’s development.
“It demonstrates the tangible results of our current teaching and research strategies, which build on liberal arts education as our traditional foundation,” he said.
THE also noted China led the region for the eighth consecutive year, with its universities occupying five of the top 10 places. All held their positions from last year except Shanghai Jiao Tong University, which rose one place to ninth.
“China has improved its overall score by making strides in several metrics, particularly patents, research excellence and research influence,” it said.

data.title: Chinese economist Fu Peng hails crypto’s historic rise as he joins Bitfire
data.description:
Chinese economist Fu Peng, whose candid remarks about the country’s economic ills went viral, has joined a Hong Kong cryptocurrency firm as chief scientist, hailing the “historical” leap of digital assets into mainstream finance despite their current slump.
“[Digital assets] have become mature enough to be added to investment portfolios,” Fu said in Hong Kong on Thursday, addressing audience members at an event organised by his new employer Bitfire Group, a Hong Kong-listed crypto asset management firm previously linked to the Chinese crypto exchange Huobi, now known as HTX.
Fu said he decided to join the crypto industry because its current moment represented the “second great integration” between finance and technology, following the emergence of the fixed income, currencies and commodities markets in the 1980s.
“I hope my speech today will go down in history,” he said.

Fu’s high-profile entry into digital assets is rare for a well-known Chinese economist, given Beijing’s ban on the crypto industry on the mainland. Meanwhile, Hong Kong is encouraging crypto activities under a strict regulatory regime.
Fu, who was the chief economist at Chinese brokerage firm Northeast Securities from 2020 to May last year, repeatedly made waves in 2024 in China with critical comments about the country’s slowing economy.
He spoke about the real estate crisis damaging consumer demand and warned that China risked facing stalled growth “like Japan over the past 35 years” if it did not tackle wealth redistribution. Fu’s WeChat account was suspended after his remarks garnered widespread attention.
Bitfire CEO Livio Weng said at the same briefing that Fu’s move to join the company echoed examples in the US, where some traditional finance industry figures in recent years had become proponents of the crypto industry, such as Kathie Wood, CEO of fund manager Ark Invest, and Tom Lee, head of research at Fundstrat Global Advisors.
Fu said he would focus on providing macro research for Bitfire’s clients instead of publishing public-facing research reports.
Bitfire said that it had acquired a trading system and hired a team of 20 people from Avenir Group, the family office of Li Lin, the founder of HTX and Bitfire’s largest shareholder.
The US$1.6 million acquisition would allow the firm to offer its clients bitcoin-denominated investment services, Weng said.
The firm is expanding even amid a crypto sector downturn that began with a market crash in October. Bitcoin traded at around US$77,940 on Thursday, compared with its all-time high of US$126,000 in October.

data.title: Asia’s chipmakers feel the heat as naphtha crunch hits photoresist supply
data.description:
The distant geopolitical conflict in the Middle East is sending shock waves through Asia’s semiconductor industry, exposing fresh vulnerabilities in the supply chain as shortages of photoresist – a critical chipmaking material – emerge as the latest weak link.
With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed since early March, supplies of naphtha – a key feedstock for specialty chemicals used in semiconductor production including photoresist – have been sharply curtailed.
Produced during the refining of crude oil or natural gas, naphtha is essential to the manufacture of advanced chips used in applications ranging from artificial intelligence to car systems.
South Korea’s memory chip giants Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are among those most exposed.
Major Japanese photoresist suppliers had warned both firms of looming disruptions to raw material procurement, according to South Korean media outlet TheElec. The companies did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Thursday.
The shortage is expected to hit advanced nodes that rely on extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography the hardest, given the process’s sensitivity and tight tolerances.
“How long it lasts really depends on the duration of the war,” said Gary Ng, a senior economist at Natixis Corporate and Investment Bank.

Japan dominates the global photoresist market, with JSR, Tokyo Ohka Kogyo, Shin-Etsu Chemical and Fujifilm leading supply across semiconductors, displays and printed circuit boards.
These four companies accounted for 76 per cent of the global market in 2023, with an even stronger grip on high-end materials, according to Shenzhen Enterprise Investment Research, citing data from the China Electronics Materials Industry Association.
The situation underscores the fragility of Asia’s semiconductor supply chain, where chipmakers remain highly sensitive to upstream disruptions and logistical bottlenecks.
The broader technology sector is already bracing for prolonged instability in the Middle East, which could weigh on chip production and slow the expansion of AI data centres.
The shortage of PGME and PGMEA – the crucial solvents chemically refined from naphtha that make chip printing possible – was set to affect advanced nodes that use EUV lithography, according to Phelix Lee, a senior equity analyst at Morningstar.
“Semiconductor manufacturers store months of safety inventory, and coupled with partial replacements from the US, we expect a half year, or slightly more than that, is still considered ‘safe’ in the sense of maintaining chip output,” Lee added.
Taiwan and South Korea remain the largest markets for photoresist.
Taiwan – home to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) – accounted for an estimated 26.4 per cent of global demand, followed by South Korea with 25.4 per cent, according to Tokyo-based research firm Fuji Keizai.
The firm expects the global photoresist market to reach US$2.7 billion by 2026, up 9 per cent from 2025.
Joseph Chung, an analyst at Isaiah Research, said the short-term impact on TSMC may be contained.
“In terms of raw material sourcing, TSMC continues to diversify its supply strategy and has established a globally diversified supplier base,” he said, noting that major chipmakers typically maintained inventory buffers for key materials ranging from several weeks to a few months.




China’s semiconductor supply chain appears less immediately affected than South Korea’s, reflecting its focus on mature process nodes and its push for self-sufficiency.
Domestic players, including Xuzhou B&C Chemical, have been expanding capabilities in areas such as photoresist.
“Leading Chinese semiconductor companies are also demonstrating strong supply chain resilience based on our investigations,” Chung said.
The country was less affected in the sense that it had a relatively complete industrial supply chain to produce various materials and seek alternative solutions, Natixis’ Ng said.
Even so, China remains dependent on Japan for advanced materials. While local substitution efforts were under way, replacing certain inputs “may still affect yield and increase costs”, Ng added.
Japan accounted for 56 per cent of China’s imported photoresists in the first half of 2025, according to semiconductor research firm ICWise.

data.title: WeWork finds new lease of life in Hong Kong as uncertainty drives global demandCompanies pull back from traditional office commitments, driving a surge in flexible space demand as WeWork adds a new si
data.description:
Global flexible workspace operator WeWork is expanding in Hong Kong as companies scale back long-term office commitments, opening its fifth site in Causeway Bay to tap rising demand for short-term, flexible space amid economic and geopolitical uncertainty.
The new location at Soundwill Plaza II Midtown, owned by Soundwill Holdings, was previously occupied by another co-working operator. WeWork secured the site after competing with several rivals.
“In the current uncertain market outlook, landlords are looking for reliable, long-term and stable partners,” said Wang Cheung, city leader for WeWork Hong Kong and Taipei. The firm now operates four floors in the building, totalling 31,800 sq ft.
“We believe occupancy at this location will reach around 80 per cent by the end of the year,” Cheung said.
The opening reflects WeWork’s gradual rebuild in the city. The company re-entered Hong Kong in 2022 after closing several locations in 2020, and has since expanded across Hong Kong Island while eyeing new sites in Kowloon.
The Causeway Bay project targets small teams, offering more than 60 offices for groups of two to 13 people. Early demand has come from mainland Chinese firms establishing initial overseas operations. Monthly pricing starts at HK$2,800 (US$358) for hot desks and HK$4,000 per workstation for private offices.

The expansion aligns with a broader shift in demand. Data from The Instant Group shows flexible workspace demand in Hong Kong rose 27 per cent in the first two months of 2026, as companies responded to “ongoing economic uncertainty” by prioritising flexibility.
The same data showed demand was increasingly concentrated in smaller offices, particularly in the one- to nine-desk range, reflecting hybrid work and more distributed teams. Core districts continue to dominate, with Central and Admiralty accounting for about a third of demand, followed by Causeway Bay and Wan Chai.
Within Causeway Bay, WeWork’s Lee Garden One site, owned by Hysan Development, is its best-performing location, with occupancy at 92 per cent, supported by tenants across technology, finance and professional services.
WeWork said global factors were also shaping demand. “Amid ongoing geopolitical instability in the Middle East and globally, we have seen capital inflow shifting towards Hong Kong as funds seek a safe harbour,” the company said.
These trends are playing out against an uneven office market recovery.
According to JLL, overall grade A vacancy stood at 13.5 per cent at the end of March, with Central at 9.6 per cent and Wan Chai and Causeway Bay at 10.3 per cent, compared with 20.4 per cent in Kowloon East.
Rents edged up 0.1 per cent month on month in March, driven largely by Central, while Kowloon East continued to face pressure, with rents falling 0.7 per cent.
Flexible workspace pricing has remained relatively stable despite the broader downturn. Average desk rates rose about 2 per cent year on year to around US$608 per month in 2025, according to The Instant Group.
Landlords are adapting. Hysan Development has partnered with co-working operators in Causeway Bay, while CK Asset Holdings and Hang Lung Properties have launched in-house flexible workspace platforms to support occupancy.

data.title: Beijing sounds alarm over urgent need to clean up online ads
data.description:
China’s top market regulator will launch a six-month crackdown on the country’s internet advertising sector, targeting malpractices including the misuse of artificial intelligence, in what it described as its first campaign to clean up the broader online advertising ecosystem.
The State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) said on Thursday that the campaign comes as new risks – from AI misuse to traffic-driven marketing tactics – emerge alongside long-standing issues, even as data and technology continue to drive rapid growth in the sector.
China’s online advertising market was expected to reach 793 billion yuan (US$116 billion) in 2025, according to estimates by consultancy QuestMobile.
“There is an urgent need to carry out a systematic and comprehensive clean-up,” the regulator said.

As part of the campaign, SAMR said it would, for the first time, frame enforcement around the concept of the “internet advertising ecosystem”, focusing on five key areas: tightening regulation; strengthening platform accountability; overseeing new advertising formats; upgrading monitoring technology; and reinforcing content standards.
The regulator did not name specific companies. However, an August report from QuestMobile showed that three tech giants – ByteDance, Tencent Holdings and Alibaba Group Holding – together accounted for more than half of China’s online advertising market in the first half of 2025. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
SAMR said it would step up enforcement against violations that had triggered public concern, including stricter oversight of advertising embedded in livestream e-commerce, which had rapidly become a mainstream retail channel.
It also pledged tighter regulation of AI-generated advertising, as advances in Chinese AI systems enabled the creation of increasingly realistic content.

data.title: Blooming good: Monet, maestros shine as garden art bridges East and West
data.description:
Two famed water lily paintings by French Impressionist master Claude Monet will go on display in Hong Kong from Friday in a free exhibition highlighting cultural exchange between East and West.
The show, titled “Blooming: the Art of Garden in East and West”, brings together more than 100 sets of exhibits from the Art Institute of Chicago, Beijing’s Palace Museum and the Palace of Versailles in Paris.
The Hong Kong Museum of Art in Tsim Sha Tsui, which is hosting the show, has also contributed pieces.
Works by other masters including Zhang Daqian, Wen Zhengming, Wu Guanzhong will be presented alongside lacquerware, sculptures, ceramics, glassware and prints that explore the taste of the powerful across cultures.
The museum’s director, Maria Mok Kar-wing, said the exhibition was part of the museum’s latest international collaboration.
“The DNA of the museum is very Hong Kong … [with our collections showing] a diverse art world from the encounters between the East and the West, new and old,” Mok said.
“To us, [the exhibition] is one way of expressing this.”

Wang Tao, the Art Institute of Chicago’s executive director of initiatives in Asia, highlighted the importance of the curation in bringing out the theme of East-West exchange, stressing that it was more than simply “hanging up the paintings”.
“It’s about the dialogue between the works … You’d be able to see the difference between Monet and Zhang Daqian in their understanding of light and nature in the show,” Wang said, referring to the 20th century Chinese artist known for his splash-ink style.
Wang also noted a specific vantage point in the exhibition where viewers can see Monet’s Water Lily Pond painted in 1900, which depicted the artist’s famous Japanese garden that he built in Giverny, France.
He said the piece can be compared with an 18th century painting titled Thatched Hall by Huangshan by the Chinese literati Yu Zhiding, who similarly built and depicted his own garden, offering insight into differing aesthetic traditions.
The exhibition opens on Friday and runs until July 29, with free admission.

data.title: Why Anthropic’s Mythos has energised China’s cybersecurity industry
data.description:
In the second of a three-part series on Anthropic’s powerful Mythos artificial intelligence model, we examine the effect it has had on China’s cybersecurity and finance industries.
US start-up Anthropic’s new AI model, Claude Mythos Preview, has drawn global attention for its ability to autonomously identify and exploit cybersecurity vulnerabilities at a level that appears to surpass conventional tools used in enterprise and financial systems.
The model has not been made publicly available, with Anthropic instead granting limited access to selected organisations for controlled testing. While the company’s services remain unavailable in China, its release has nonetheless sparked discussion about its potential impact on China’s cybersecurity and finance industries.
Why are Chinese cybersecurity firms rallying?
In China, official reactions and public discussions around Mythos have been relatively muted compared with the rest of the world – the exception being China’s cybersecurity industry.
Following Anthropic’s Mythos announcement on April 7, shares of major Chinese cybersecurity firms including Qi An Xin, Sangfor Technologies and 360 Security Technology rose for several consecutive days, reflecting growing expectations that demand for AI-driven cybersecurity and compliance solutions could accelerate.
Austin Zhao, a senior research manager at IDC China, said the strong market reaction highlighted the sector’s close attention to Mythos, adding that while its capabilities had been anticipated, the model’s actual performance still surprised many.
The technology could drive up cybersecurity costs as firms increase spending on personnel, infrastructure and advanced protection systems, while also creating new opportunities for AI-based security services, said James Gong, legal director at the law firm Bird & Bird.
Recent developments in China also point to growing capabilities in AI-driven vulnerability discovery. For example, 360 Security Technology claimed it had developed an AI-powered “vulnerability discovery agent” that identified hundreds of previously unknown flaws, including in widely used software such as Microsoft Office, according to a report published on Wednesday by Chinese cybersecurity research group Natto Thoughts.

What are China’s tech giants doing?
Chinese “big tech” firms were a major presence at this year’s C3 Security Conference, one of China’s biggest annual cybersecurity industry conferences, which was held in Chengdu over the weekend.
Notably, they announced several industry initiatives in partnership with major cybersecurity firms, including a tie-up between Huawei Technologies and leading Chinese cybersecurity vendor Asiainfo Security to bolster efforts to protect Huawei’s HarmonyOS operating system.
Meanwhile, Alibaba Cloud launched a consortium with industry partners at the conference to coordinate research work on AI agent security, focusing on reliable application of AI models in fields such as finance and healthcare.
Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Ma Hongjun, CEO of Asiainfo Security, warned in remarks at the conference that in the near future most of today’s cybersecurity products and tools could become ineffective, as both attackers and defenders increasingly shifted towards autonomous AI agents operating at rapid speed.
He added that the risks introduced by AI would ultimately need to be addressed by AI itself, highlighting an emerging paradigm in which “AI is used to defend against AI-driven threats”.

What has the reaction been in China’s finance industry?
The emergence of Mythos has especially drawn attention to the immediate risks posed to the worldwide financial industry, given its central role in the global economy.
Against this backdrop, the cautious stance of Chinese financial institutions towards integrating external AI systems reflects broader compliance obligations and risk management considerations.
This contrasts with the more immediate responses seen globally, where officials in the US, Europe and Asia have already convened discussions with financial institutions following the release of Mythos to assess potential risks to critical financial infrastructure.
China’s financial system has long taken a cautious approach to integrating AI into core operations, prioritising financial stability and risk control over rapid adoption. Regulators and major institutions have generally limited the use of AI to peripheral or experimental functions, amid concerns over the reliability and controllability of advanced models.
At the Global Wealth Management Forum 2025, Zhu Yunlai, former president and chief executive of China International Capital Corporation, warned that AI could represent the most significant technological shock since financial digitalisation, raising issues including data security risks, gaps in oversight and ambiguous valuation frameworks.
He added that challenges such as imprecise model reasoning and difficulty in constraining behavioural boundaries underscored the need for financial institutions to prioritise market stability when adopting AI technologies, reinforcing the sector’s traditionally conservative stance in China.

data.title: Hong Kong airport’s revenue ‘expected to grow by up to 10%’ despite Mideast war
data.description:
The authority managing the Hong Kong International Airport expects revenue to grow by up to 10 per cent this year despite ongoing disruptions arising from the Iran war, its CEO has said, adding that she aims to position the facility as an alternative aviation hub to the Middle East.
In a wide-ranging interview with the South China Morning Post on Thursday, Airport Authority Hong Kong CEO Vivian Cheung Kar-fay said the flight cancellations triggered by the conflict, including those by flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways, were “less desirable” to travellers hoping to fly to the region.
Cheung added that the authority was working to attract airlines to the city to launch flights.
“Some aircraft might not be able to stop by, or fall out of, the Middle East, and they might choose other places, and Hong Kong can be a great starting point for them to launch their flights,” she said on the sidelines of the Singapore Yachting Festival.
“That is something we will work on for the coming years. Of course, hopefully the war will end soon, and oil prices will come down, but even with the current status, we’re working on other alternatives.”
Cheung said that Hong Kong airport was expected to welcome about 70 million passengers this year, an increase from around 61 million last year, despite flight cancellations caused by the conflict.
She shared the prediction despite Cathay Group’s earlier decision to cut flights for its main airline by 2 per cent and by 6 per cent for its budget arm, HK Express, between mid-May and the end of June.
Both carriers have vowed to restore flights to the Middle East after June.

Cheung said Cathay was likely to have chosen to suspend routes that were less popular as the company would seek to maintain its passenger capacity.
“Honestly speaking, I think they are all good businesspeople. Cathay is a good business company, and the flights that they cancel would definitely be the ones that are less desirable – not ones to destinations that are people’s top choices,” she said.
“They will not cancel any flights to popular destinations, but more to areas that fewer people choose.”
Cheung also forecast that the airport’s revenue would grow by 9 to 10 per cent year on year in 2026, adding that she expected it to be another profitable year after taking into consideration costs related to the imminent launch of the Terminal 2 facility next month.
“The net profit is similar to last year,” she said.
Cheung shared the forecast just days after the authority announced it was recruiting interested parties to invest in a mega marina project at the airport, as part of its broader HK$100 billion Airport City development.
The 200-hectare marina will include Hong Kong’s largest yachting bay, as well as hospitality and recreational facilities such as water sports zones. The first phase of the facility is scheduled for completion in 2028.
Cheung said the authority had received about 20 inquiries since it started inviting investors and partners to submit expressions of interest on Monday. She added that interested parties came from Hong Kong, mainland China and around the world.
The CEO said the Hong Kong government felt that it was an opportune time to enter the marine economy, on the back of high local and global demand.
According to the authority, the city currently has 12,500 licensed yachts – including about 90 superyachts with lengths of 30 metres or more – but just 4,300 berths.
The figure puts Hong Kong second in the Asia-Pacific region in terms of the number of superyachts, only lagging behind Australia.
The authority also said the value of the global yachting market was projected to reach US$45 billion by 2032.
“We are capturing the opportunity, deregulating many areas surrounding the economy and providing spaces for yacht bays,” Cheung said, adding that Hong Kong had edged out its surrounding competitors with its tax-free policies.

She said the project aimed to attract people from Hong Kong and the rest of the Greater Bay Area, as well as tourists transiting through Hong Kong or travelling to the city.
The airport’s marina project will provide 500 berths for yachts, including spaces for vessels measuring more than 100 metres in length.
Suzy Rayment, the executive director of the Asia-Pacific Superyacht Association, said the marina would be a relief for the yacht industry.
Boats were getting larger, and explorer yachts about 120 metres long were no longer uncommon, she said, noting the larger berths would cater to those superyacht owners.
“All the marinas are full, and people in the industry were crying out for more marinas,” Rayment said.
She added that Hong Kong marinas were popular among yacht owners from the rest of the Greater Bay Area, who were often deterred from docking in mainland Chinese waters due to evolving regulations.
“If we want to be the biggest, then we should also provide space for the biggest yachts,” Cheung said. “When they come to this part of the world, they will look for a city that has a facility for them to dock, and we will have it.”
The authority aims to start the tendering process for the project early next year, with the first phase scheduled for completion in 2028, in line with multiple other Skytopia projects, such as the AsiaWorld-Expo Phase 2 expansion and the Airport-Tung Chung Link.
Cheung also touched on the 11 Skies mall development project, saying the authority was working with developer New World Development to complete the initiative under the same timeline. The property giant is currently facing debt and liquidity issues.
“Of course, they have a lot of challenges to overcome. We definitely have the best hopes for them, and I think they will survive through this crisis, and so we’re still working with them and looking at many options,” she said.

data.title: Hong Kong airport staff offered free measles jabs in bid to prevent outbreak
data.description:
Health authorities will provide free measles vaccinations to Hong Kong airport workers from Friday, following the detection of three cases among aircraft maintenance staff.
The Centre for Health Protection (CHP) warned on Thursday of the “relatively high” risk of measles transmission at the aviation hub, citing the high volume of travellers passing through there and the sizeable proportion of non-local-born workers who might not have received measles jabs in childhood.
“In order to prevent another measles outbreak among airport staff, the centre will set up a vaccination booth at the airport starting [on Friday] to provide local airport staff with free measles vaccination,” it said.
“The CHP and the Airport Authority Hong Kong will coordinate and arrange for eligible airport staff from various companies to visit the vaccination booth in an orderly manner.”
The drive aimed to strengthen herd immunity in the airport community of about 2,500 employees – including 900 not born in Hong Kong – and to safeguard public health, it added.

Epidemiological data collected showed that about 30 per cent of employees were uncertain of their immunity status against measles.
Health authorities recently revealed a measles cluster involving three back-end support staff responsible for aircraft repair and maintenance.
Investigations showed that the source of infection was a case confirmed on April 6, involving a patient who travelled to Indonesia during the incubation period and was believed to have contracted the virus there. The other two patients were on duty on the same shift.
“It is highly likely that they were infected with the measles virus at work, constituting a cluster of workplace transmissions triggered by an imported case,” centre controller Dr Edwin Tsui Lok-kin said.
“None of the three patients in this outbreak knew whether they had been vaccinated against measles.”
Tsui urged all Hong Kong International Airport staff – especially those born outside the city – to review their vaccination records as soon as possible.
People born and raised in Hong Kong before 1967 could be considered to have acquired immunity to measles through natural infection, as the disease was endemic in the city at that time.
The Hong Kong Childhood Immunisation Programme introduced a measles vaccine for the first time in 1967.
